messages.content: Is Numpy or Sympy allow for stage 2 solver.py submission?
messages.subject: Third party packages for Stage 2

messages.content: Yes, NumPy or SymPy can be used in the Stage 2 solver.py submission. This does not violate the fairness of the competition.

messages.subject: Third party packages for Stage 2

messages.content:
@Gan W. Thanks, good point. We will include NumPy.

More generally, third-party packages can be added to the sandbox as long as they do not affect fairness, reproducibility, or security.

Please propose any packages you would like to use in this Zulip topic. For specific requests, opening a GitHub issue is also welcome.

We will share the approved package list with versions.

messages.content:
Hi, building on Gan W.'s question — for solvers that need to do
non-trivial counterexample search, the following packages would
be valuable:

  • z3-solver: SMT-based search for finite magmas satisfying Eq1
    but violating Eq2. ETP itself uses Z3 (generate_z3_counterexample
    in teorth/equational_theories). Most direct path to FALSE
    certificates beyond brute-force enumeration.

  • networkx: graph operations on the implication poset. The ETP
    graph has ~22M edges and reasoning about ancestry/transitive
    closure is natural in this representation.

  • pysat (alternative): if z3-solver is rejected for size or
    fairness reasons, pure-Python SAT.

For all three, deterministic seeding is straightforward and the
output is a finite witness that the Lean judge can verify
independently. Reproducibility is preserved.

Versions I'd target: z3-solver==4.13.0, networkx==3.3, pysat==1.8.dev1.

If the team has fairness concerns about z3 specifically (some
participants having local Z3 expertise), a documented allowlist
of which Z3 APIs are permitted would address that without
removing the tool entirely.

messages.subject: Third party packages for Stage 2

messages.content:
Screenshot 2026-05-09 at 10.05.46 AM.png
Hi @YZ and community,                                                                                                           

I'm trying to submit my Stage 2 solver to the Playground and Contributor Network but running into issues:

1. Playground: I can't seem to load my solver.py into the Stage 2 Playground for testing. Is there a specific upload flow or authorization?

I'm missing, or is the Playground currently only accepting certain formats? Is there a way I could run it?

2. Contributor Network: When I try to share my solver on the Stage 2 Contributor Network (https://competition.sair.foundation/contributor-network?competition=mathematics-distillation-challenge-equational-theories-stage2), it doesn't seem to gothrough.

My solver is a single solver.py file (232KB, well under 500KB limit). It handles both Solo and Marathon tracks (auto-detects via JUDGE_MARATHON_MANIFEST env var). It includes:

- 22M implication matrix oracle (108KB compressed)

- 27 hardcoded proofs for the hardest cases

- 13-level tactic engine (grind, self-app, Tao synthesis, Ježek h.symm, etc.)

- Full counterexample engine (structured, affine, Brockian, product Z_p×Z_q, cyclic successor, random, backtracking)

- LLM fallback with judge feedback

Tested locally against all public benchmarks: 918/919 true (99.9%), 922/950 false (97.1%) = 1840/1869 (98.4%).

Any guidance on the correct submission/sharing flow for Stage 2 would be appreciated. Thanks!

— Christopher Brock / Riemann Labs
messages.subject: Third party packages for Stage 2

messages.content:
@Christopher Brock Thanks for the detailed note.

At the moment, the Stage 2 Playground is only open to participants who registered for Stage 1. We are considering opening it more broadly so that others can also use the Playground to check format and run basic tests, while giving more credit/priority to participants who registered for Stage 1.

The Stage 2 Contributor Network should be working now. If you still run into upload or sharing issues, please DM me and we can dive into the details.
messages.subject: Third party packages for Stage 2

messages.content:
Thanks @Vincent Witte - I hope to share in the network soon. I have submitted my latest version and am down to 29 hard problems. I even built a digital overview of the process and idea. happy to share.

@YZ - The stage 2 contribution area to share work is based on pushing the python script in the playground first. I have my submission in but cannot move further nor share. I didn't know about the stage 1 competition or I would have partipated as well.

messages.content:
Hi @YZ , following up on Christopher Brock’s message about Lean-side tactic engines for Stage 2 solvers.

Could you clarify whether submitted Lean certificates are allowed to use standard Lean 4 / Mathlib tactics such as grind, simp, omega, aesop, etc., assuming the generated code contains no sorry/admit and passes the deterministic judge?

In other words, is the policy based on the declarations/axioms used by the elaborated proof, or are some tactics themselves disallowed in submitted certificate code?

This is separate from Python-side packages like z3 or networkx; I’m asking only about tactics available inside the Lean code submitted to the judge.
messages.subject: Third party packages for Stage 2

edit_history.prev_content:
Hi @YZ, following up on Christopher Brock’s message about Lean-side tactic engines for Stage 2 solvers.

Could you clarify whether submitted Lean certificates are allowed to use standard Lean 4 / Mathlib tactics such as `grind`, `simp`, `omega`, `aesop`, etc., assuming the generated code contains no `sorry`/`admit` and passes the deterministic judge?

In other words, is the policy based on the declarations/axioms used by the elaborated proof, or are some tactics themselves disallowed in submitted certificate code?

This is separate from Python-side packages like `z3` or `networkx`; I’m asking only about tactics available inside the Lean code submitted to the judge.
edit_history.prev_rendered_content:
Hi @YZ, following up on Christopher Brock’s message about Lean-side tactic engines for Stage 2 solvers.

Could you clarify whether submitted Lean certificates are allowed to use standard Lean 4 / Mathlib tactics such as grind, simp, omega, aesop, etc., assuming the generated code contains no sorry/admit and passes the deterministic judge?

In other words, is the policy based on the declarations/axioms used by the elaborated proof, or are some tactics themselves disallowed in submitted certificate code?

This is separate from Python-side packages like z3 or networkx; I’m asking only about tactics available inside the Lean code submitted to the judge.

messages.content: Yes. Submitted proofs may use standard Lean 4 / Mathlib tactics, including simp, grind, omega, aesop, and decide. The policy is dependency-based, not tactic-name-based: the judge inspects the elaborated proof and checks the axioms and direct declarations it actually uses against the configured allowlists (ProofPolicy.allowed_axioms, allowed_declarations, and allowed_declaration_prefixes in judge/verify.py). The only source-level bans are proof holes (sorry, admit, sorryAx, mkSorry), debug output (dbg_trace, dbgTrace), elaboration-time IO and metaprogramming (run_tac, initialize, builtin_initialize, #eval, #exit, #reduce, #synth, #check_eval, elab, elab_rules, macro, macro_rules, syntax), and kernel-bypass constructs (unsafe, implemented_by, extern, unsafeCast, unsafeIO, unsafePerformIO). See BANNED_PROOF_TOKENS in judge/verify.py for the authoritative list. A simp- or grind-based proof can still be rejected if it pulls in a lemma outside the allowed declarations, but that rejection is about the dependency, not the tactic itself.
